startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 38-year-old primigravida is concerned about the risk of Down syndrome. Work through this case exploring prenatal screening methods, diagnostic procedures, and the PCPNDT Act.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly identified prenatal screening and diagnostic approaches, including NIPT sensitivity and the PCPNDT Act.
You showed some understanding but missed key concepts. Review NIPT vs amniocentesis timing, serum marker patterns, and the PCPNDT Act.
Revisit prenatal screening markers, NIPT mechanism, amniocentesis vs CVS timing, and Indian laws on sex determination.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 38-year-old primigravida at 12 weeks of gestation is anxious about the risk of chromosomal abnormalities, particularly Down syndrome (trisomy 21). She asks about available screening and diagnostic options.

You explain that NIPT (non-invasive prenatal testing) detects cell-free fetal DNA in maternal blood and has a sensitivity of 99% for trisomy 21. It can be performed as early as 10 weeks.

You also counsel her about the PCPNDT Act of 1994, which prohibits sex determination for the purpose of sex-selective abortion in India.

branchingQuestion.question: The patient's NIPT is positive for trisomy 21. She wants a confirmatory diagnostic test. Which invasive procedure can be performed earliest, and at what gestational age?

alternatives.text: Chorionic villus sampling (CVS) at 10-12 weeks — earlier than amniocentesis
Amniocentesis at 10-12 weeks for earliest diagnosis

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: Amniocentesis is typically performed at 15-18 weeks, not 10-12 weeks. CVS can be done earlier at 10-12 weeks of gestation for first-trimester diagnosis.

alternatives.text: Cordocentesis at 10-12 weeks via fetal blood sampling

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Cordocentesis (fetal blood sampling) is performed after 18 weeks and is reserved for specific indications. CVS at 10-12 weeks is the earliest confirmatory diagnostic test.

params.text:
Further Investigation

Correct! CVS can be performed at 10-12 weeks, earlier than amniocentesis (15-18 weeks).

You review the patient's first-trimester screening results. In Down syndrome screening, serum AFP is decreased and hCG is increased. Combined with nuchal translucency measurement on ultrasound, this gives a risk estimate.

The patient asks whether CVS or amniocentesis would be better at this stage:

  • CVS: 10-12 weeks — samples chorionic villi (placental tissue)
  • Amniocentesis: 15-18 weeks — samples amniotic fluid containing fetal cells

branchingQuestion.question: In the serum screening for Down syndrome, what is the pattern of AFP and hCG levels?

alternatives.text: AFP is decreased and hCG is increased in Down syndrome
Both AFP and hCG are increased in Down syndrome

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: In Down syndrome, AFP is decreased (not increased) while hCG is elevated. Remember: AFP down, hCG up in trisomy 21.

alternatives.text: Both AFP and hCG are decreased in Down syndrome

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: In Down syndrome, hCG is elevated, not decreased. The pattern is AFP decreased + hCG increased. This is opposite to neural tube defects where AFP is elevated.

params.text:
Clinical Synthesis

Excellent! Key prenatal diagnosis concepts:

  • NIPT: Detects cell-free fetal DNA in maternal blood; 99% sensitivity for trisomy 21
  • CVS: 10-12 weeks (earliest invasive test)
  • Amniocentesis: 15-18 weeks
  • Down syndrome markers: AFP decreased, hCG increased
  • PCPNDT Act (1994): Prohibits sex determination for sex-selective abortion in India
